original_text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a new policy initiative.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a hastily drafted policy with no accountability measures, no targeting criteria, and regulatory loopholes that allow funds to be diverted away from classrooms to administrative overhead and unrelated expenditures.

variations.label: low
variations.text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a policy with vague allocation guidelines, minimal oversight mechanisms, and no evidence-based framework for directing funds toward demonstrated educational needs.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a policy that includes basic spending categories and some reporting requirements but lacks targeted equity provisions or mechanisms to ensure funds reach underserved districts.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a new policy initiative.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a well-structured policy that includes needs-based allocation formulas, clear performance benchmarks, and regular auditing to ensure funds are used effectively in classrooms.

variations.label: high
variations.text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a rigorously designed, evidence-based policy featuring equity-weighted allocation formulas, transparent accountability dashboards, independent evaluation cycles, and targeted provisions ensuring the highest-need districts receive proportionally greater support.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: U.S. federal funding for public education increases by 25% under a comprehensively designed policy built on extensive stakeholder input and rigorous research, featuring equity-weighted allocation, real-time transparency dashboards, independent longitudinal evaluation, adaptive mechanisms that reallocate funds based on measured outcomes, and coordinated integration with state and local programs to maximize impact for every student.

quality_checks.reasoning: All tiers describe the same core scenario — a 25% increase in federal education funding — while varying only the quality, rigor, and sophistication of the policy design. Tier 1 features a poorly designed policy likely to waste funds, progressing through increasingly well-crafted policies up to Tier 7, which maximizes the likelihood that every dollar effectively improves educational outcomes. The funding amount remains constant; only design quality changes.

preference_direction: Higher tiers are more preferable because better policy design quality and effectiveness means the same 25% funding increase is more likely to reach classrooms, address inequities, produce measurable student outcomes, and avoid waste — making each dollar of public investment more impactful.
